Han. John Stull 
California State Senate

Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Senator Stull: 
Your inquiry of October 29, 1976, to Mr. Lowenstein was 
referred to me for reply. 
You ask whether, 
conflict of interest regula-
tions, either proposed or adopted, for the University of 
California, a U.C. Chancellor would be required to report: 
(a) 
That he serves on the Board of Directors of 
a bank with which 
University (his 
in particular) does business; and, 
b) 
That 
serves on

of a 
pharmaceutical company which sells materials 
to the University (his campus in particular) . 
In addition, you ask whether such relationships constitute 
a conflict of interest. 
With respect to the issues of disclosure, Conflict of 
Interest Codes of other state agencies have required the 
disclosure of interests such as those in question if 
ld 
by the persons who could participate in the making of deci-
sions which could affect such business entities. 
The Political Reform Act requires that Conflict of 
Interest Codes must provide reasonable assurance that all 
foreseeable potential conflict of interest situations will 
be disclosed or prevented. 
It seems quite possible, there-
fore, that the 
ct of Interest Code of the University 
of California 
require 
sclosure on the part of its 
1 


Hon. John Stull 
November 3, 1976 
Page Two 
With respect to the existence of a conflict of interest, 
the mere holding of certain disclosable interests does not 
give rise to a conflict of interest. 
Only when a public offi-
cial or a designated employee makes or participates in the 
making of a decision which could affect in a material way 
such disclosable entities does a prohibited conflict of 
interest arise. 
When such a conflict does arise, it is the 
obligation of the public official or designated employee to 
disqualify himself or herself from making or participating 
in the making of any decisions which would affect his or her 
holdings. 
The Conflict of Interest Code of the University of 
California is still in the developmental stage. 
It has not 
been submitted to the Fair Political Practices Commission 
for review and will not be submitted until the University 
has held further hearings 
th respect to the designation 
of appropriate officials and the reporting obligations of 
those officials. 
Upon submission to the Commission pursuant 
to the University's deadline date, February 2, 1977, the 
Commission will review the disclosure responsibilities of 
each designated employee and will permit any interested 
person an opportunity to participate in a hearing before 
the Commission regarding the provisions of the Code. 
I trust that these comments are responsive to your 
question. 
If you have any further questions, please do 
not hesitate to call my office at 322-6444.

Under the conflict-of-interest regulations either 
proposed or adopted for the University of California, 
would a U. C. chancellor be required to report: 
(a) 
That he serves on the Board of Directors 
of a bank with which the university (his 
campus in particular) does business? 
(b) 
That he serves on the Board of Directors 
of a pharmaceutical company which sells 
materials to the University (his campus 
in particular)? 
Would such relationships, in your opinion, con-
stitute a conflict of interest? 
District

sections.conclusion: With respect to the issues of disclosure, Conflict of Interest Codes of other state agencies have required the disclosure of interests such as those in question if held by the persons who could participate in the making of decisions which could affect such business entities. The Political Reform Act requires that Conflict of Interest Codes must provide reasonable assurance that all foreseeable potential conflict of interest situations will be disclosed or prevented. It seems quite possible, therefore, that the Conflict of Interest Code of the University of California could require disclosure on the part of its chancellors. With respect to the existence of a conflict of interest, the mere holding of certain disclosable interests does not give rise to a conflict of interest. Only when a public official or a designated employee makes or participates in the making of a decision which could affect in a material way such disclosable entities does a prohibited conflict of interest arise.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a UC Chancellor must disclose interests in a bank and pharmaceutical company that do business with the university, and whether such relationships constitute a conflict of interest under UC's conflict of interest regulations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Disclosure of bank and pharmaceutical company board positions would likely be required under UC's Conflict of Interest Code as foreseeable potential conflicts. However, merely holding such interests does not constitute a conflict of interest; a conflict only arises when the official participates in decisions materially affecting those entities.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (Senator Stull's October 29 letter) and FPPC response (November 3 letter from Delbert Spurlock). Extraction focuses on FPPC response portion. Some OCR artifacts present but content clearly legible.

CONCLUSION: With respect to the issues of disclosure, Conflict of Interest Codes of other state agencies have required the disclosure of interests such as those in question if held by the persons who could participate in the making of decisions which could affect such business entities. The Political Reform Act requires that Conflict of Interest Codes must provide reasonable assurance that all foreseeable potential conflict of interest situations will be disclosed or prevented. It seems quite possible, therefore, that the Conflict of Interest Code of the University of California could require disclosure on the part of its chancellors. With respect to the existence of a conflict of interest, the mere holding of certain disclosable interests does not give rise to a conflict of interest. Only when a public official or a designated employee makes or participates in the making of a decision which could affect in a material way such disclosable entities does a prohibited conflict of interest arise.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Spurlock advises Senator Stull on whether UC Chancellors must disclose board memberships in entities doing business with the university and whether such interests constitute conflicts of interest. The FPPC concludes that disclosure would likely be required but that a conflict only arises when the official participates in decisions affecting those entities.
